title: Inflation warning for business tax plan
full_text:
Jim Chalmers has been warned that his plan to lift the speed limit of the economy and rescue business investment from crashing via Covid-era tax relief measures, must be offset with savings to avoid piling pressure on inflation.
The Australian revealed on Thursday the Albanese government will offer a temporary tax relief package that allows businesses to “carry back” tax losses in previous years and claim refunds from the Tax Office.
The move aims to cushion businesses through an economic slowdown forecast by the Reserve Bank earlier in the week, which said business investment slumping to 0.8 per cent by the end of the year. However, economists said the policy could spur inflation if it is not accompanied by savings.
Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said the measure could boost investment and productivity in combination with an increased cap on the R&D tax break and a permanent instant asset write-off incentive, but would need to be matched with savings in the budget.
“If they can introduce a policy like this, but also make savings elsewhere, then that would be OK in terms of not putting upward pressure on inflation,” he said.
“The government has, over recent budgets, talked mostly about gross savings – that money’s then being spent elsewhere. And so there hasn’t been a net saving to the budget.
“In the current environment, it’s really important that in this budget there are net savings. You can’t just save somewhere and spend it somewhere else. That doesn’t count as a saving.” The Treasurer on Thursday flagged Tuesday’s budget would contain a “substantial productivity package”, noting that business investment was central to his plans to lift the speed limit on the economy.
“The best way to shift productivity in the right direction after two decades of underperformance is to get what the economists call this capital deepening,” Dr Chalmers told Sky News.
“That means more investment in things like the transformation, the technological revolution, the changing composition of our industrial base. We need to lift the speed limit on the economy; that’s why there’s been a lot of focus on tax, that’s understandable … but there will also be a very substantial productivity package.” The RBA in its statement of monetary policy on Tuesday forecast a mammoth hit to business investment off the back of the Iran-war driven oil supply crunch, which would leave growth at just 0.8 per cent by the end of the year – down from 3.9 per cent.
The bank’s revised figures also showed headline inflation popping to 4.8 per cent by next month, higher than its previous estimate but well below Treasury’s expectation that inflation will climb higher than 5 per cent.
Westpac chief economist Pat Bustamante on Thursday said both state and federal fiscal policy would remain stimulatory into the next financial year even before considering recently announced and prospective support for business and households during the global oil shock.
“The national fiscal impulse is slightly expansionary in FY2027, even before factoring in additional policy support expected in upcoming state and federal budgets,” Mr Bustamante said.
“Given the ongoing pipeline of policy measures and the political imperative to support households during this oil price shock, there is a clear risk that fiscal settings remain more expansionary than currently assumed.” The Australian on Tuesday revealed the Albanese government was poised to announce a multibillion-dollar cash splash for workers as its centrepiece cost-of-living support in the upcoming budget. KPMG’s Mid-market Tax Advisory partner-in-charge Kaylene Hubbard said the temporary measure would be a positive boost for the “engine room of the economy” when combined with other structural reforms.

summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has been advised that his proposed temporary tax relief for businesses must be balanced with budget savings to prevent inflationary pressures. The Albanese government aims to stimulate business investment amid a forecasted economic slowdown, but economists warn that without net savings, inflation could rise. The Reserve Bank of Australia has also revised its inflation forecast, indicating potential challenges ahead.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The government plans to offer temporary tax relief allowing businesses to carry back tax losses.
Economists stress the need for budget savings to offset potential inflation from the tax relief measures.
The RBA has forecasted a significant decline in business investment and an increase in inflation.
relevance: Understanding the government's fiscal strategies and their implications on inflation is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing economic risks.
